questions.prompt: Complete: "When I was a child, I ___ hate vegetables."
questions.explanation: "hate" is a state verb, so only "used to" works here: I used to hate vegetables. "would" cannot be used with state verbs like hate, and "was/got used to" means accustomed to, which is a different structure.

questions.prompt: Complete: "Every evening, my father ___ read us a story."
questions.explanation: Reading a story is a repeated past action (not a state), so "would" is correct: my father would read us a story. "be/get used to" means accustomed and takes -ing after it, which is a completely different idea.

options.text: I would have a dog when I was young.
She used to be very quiet as a teenager.
Did you used to live here?
We would know each other at school.

questions.explanation: "be" is a state verb, so only "used to" works: She used to be very quiet. "would have" and "would know" are wrong because have and know are state verbs. "Did you used to" is wrong — the -d drops in questions: Did you use to.

questions.prompt: Complete: "She is ___ working night shifts now."
questions.explanation: "be used to + -ing" expresses being accustomed: she is used to working. The full phrase here is "used to working". "used to" alone (without be) is the past-habit structure, and "get used to" means becoming accustomed, not already there.

questions.prompt: What is the KEY difference between "I used to work late" and "I am used to working late"?

options.text: The first is present, the second is past.
The first describes a past habit (now stopped); the second means I am accustomed to it now.
They mean exactly the same thing.
The first is more formal than the second.

questions.explanation: "used to work late" describes a past habit that has stopped. "am used to working late" means it is normal for me now — I am accustomed to it. They look similar but have very different meanings: past habit vs present familiarity.
